plainMeaning: May those supreme liberated ones of the world — whom we praise, bow to, and worship — grant us health, the gift of true insight, and the highest peace of mind.
recitation: kittiya vandiya mahiyā, je e logassa uttamā siddhā; ārugga-bohilābhaṁ, samāhi-varam-uttamaṁ diṁtu

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kittiya < Sanskrit kīrtita, past-passive participle of √kṛt/kīrt 'to proclaim, praise'; cf. kīrtana.
wordByWord.notes: First of a devotional triad — homage by speech.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vandiya < Sanskrit vandita, ppp of √vand 'to salute, bow'.
wordByWord.notes: Homage by the body — physical bowing.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mahiya < Sanskrit mahita, ppp of √mah 'to magnify, honour'; cf. mahā-.
wordByWord.notes: Homage by reverent worship (often glossed as the heart's pūjā), completing the triad of word, body and mind.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit je < Sanskrit ye, nominative plural of the relative ya-.
Prakrit e < Sanskrit ete 'these'; deictic to the Tīrthaṅkaras just named in vv.2–5.
wordByWord.notes: Some commentators read e as a metrical/emphatic particle rather than a demonstrative.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit loga < Sanskrit loka 'world'; genitive singular -ssa < Sanskrit -sya.
Sanskrit uttama, superlative formed on ud- 'up'; nominative plural.
Sanskrit siddha, ppp of √sidh 'to be accomplished, perfected'; nominative plural.
wordByWord.notes: The petition now addresses the praised Jinas as Siddhas — the liberated, the very goal.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ārugga < Sanskrit ārogya 'freedom from disease' (a-roga).
wordByWord.notes: Read within the compound ārugga-bohilābhaṁ; traditionally the soul's true 'health', i.e. liberation (mokṣa), not merely bodily wellness.

wordByWord.gloss: the gain of bodhi (enlightenment / right insight)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bohi < Sanskrit bodhi 'awakening' + lābha 'gain' (< √labh); accusative singular.
wordByWord.notes: bodhi here ≈ samyag-darśana (right faith); commonly understood as a prayer to secure right insight birth after birth.

wordByWord.etymology: samāhi (< Sanskrit samādhi 'composure, absorption') + vara 'excellent, boon' + uttama 'highest'; accusative singular.
wordByWord.notes: Often taken as samādhi-maraṇa — equanimity sustained calmly to the final breath.

wordByWord.gloss: may they grant, may they give
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit diṁtu < Sanskrit dadantu / diśantu, 3rd person plural imperative of √dā 'to give'.
wordByWord.notes: Variant reading dentu; carries optative force — 'may [the Siddhas] bestow'.

literalTranslation: Praised, venerated, worshipped — those who are these supreme Siddhas of the world — may they grant the gaining of health-and-enlightenment, and the most excellent, supreme samādhi.
idiomaticTranslation: Those supreme liberated souls of the world — extolled in praise, honoured with bows, worshipped in reverence — may they grant us the soul's true health, the attainment of right insight, and the highest, most excellent serenity of mind.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where verse 5 ended in a single broad plea — 'may the Jinavaras be gracious to me' (pasīyantu) — this verse makes the petition exact. It opens with a triad of past-passive participles, kittiya–vandiya–mahiyā, that the tradition reads as three ascending modes of homage: praise voiced in words, the body lowered in a bow, and the heart's worship. Devotion is thus offered through speech, body and mind together before anything is asked. Only then comes the object of devotion — je e logassa uttamā siddhā, 'these, who are the world's supreme perfected ones' — the address pointedly shifting from 'Tīrthaṅkara' and 'Jina' to siddhā, naming the worshipped ones as the very goal the reciter seeks. The second half names three boons in a deliberate ascent: ārugga (health), bohi-lābha (the gaining of enlightening insight), and samāhi-vara-uttama (the supreme, most excellent composure). Read as the compound ārugga-bohilābhaṁ, the first two fuse: the 'health' sought is not the body's but the soul's — liberation itself — and the bodhi prayed for is right faith carried forward into future births. The verb diṁtu ('may they grant') keeps the whole in the optative of humble request. Notice the shape of the asking: nothing worldly is named — not wealth, not long life, not success — only spiritual health, right insight, and a mind that stays composed. The verse teaches petition by example, modelling what a Jain may legitimately beg of the liberated: the means of liberation, and the steadiness to die well.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The three gifts encode a Jain soteriological ladder. Ārogya is glossed as the 'health' that is mokṣa — the soul free of the disease of karma; bohi (samyag-darśana, right faith) is the indispensable seed of that freedom, which the reciter asks to retain through successive births; and samāhi points to samādhi-maraṇa, the ideal of a calm, conscious, equanimous death — the same impulse that underlies sallekhanā. A doctrinal tension surfaces here: the Siddhas are vītarāga, utterly detached, and literally grant nothing. Tradition resolves the optative diṁtu by treating the liberated ones as the instrumental occasion (nimitta) of the fruit, which actually ripens from the worshipper's own act of kittiya-vandiya-mahiyā — venerating perfection orients the soul toward it.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The verse trains the reciter in what to want. Before asking, offer the full threefold homage — sincere words, a humble bow, a worshipping heart — then request only soul-health, right insight, and equanimity, especially the composure to meet life's end and one's final breath calmly. It reframes daily prayer away from worldly petitions toward the means of liberation.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava), gāthā 6, within the Second Āvaśyaka
bodhi as samyag-darśana (right faith) in Jain usage

sources.title: Bodhi — definitions (Jainism / Prakrit section)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of bodhi as awakening / right insight (samyag-darśana) and its Prakrit form bohi.

sources.title: Samadhi — definitions (Jainism section)
sources.consultedFor: Range of samādhi as composed absorption/equanimity, supporting the samāhi-vara-uttama gloss.

sources.title: Pratikramana
sources.consultedFor: Placement of the Caturviṁśati-stava (Chauvisantho) honouring the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras among the six āvaśyakas.

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Doctrine of the Tīrthaṅkara as ford-maker and of liberation (mokṣa/siddhi) underlying ārogya and the address to the Siddhas.

notes: The compound ārugga-bohilābhaṁ admits two parsings: (a) a single dvandva 'health-and-bodhi-attainment', or (b) ārogya read as a qualifier of bodhi-lābha ('the bodhi that is [the soul's] health'); the traditional Jain reading takes ārogya as the soul's health = liberation, which this rendering follows while preserving both glosses. 'e' may be demonstrative ('these') or a metrical/emphatic particle. diṁtu has a variant dentu. The verbatim Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) orthography of the source book is retained (e.g. આરુગ્ગ, સમાહિવરમુત્તમં); mātrās, anusvāras and conjuncts follow that edition. No printed Anchalgachchha English commentary on this exact gāthā was independently verified online; the dictionary and encyclopaedic sources above were used to ground the lexical and doctrinal claims.
